justification: All pass criteria met: (1) Jurisdiction-specific rules identified for all 3 jurisdictions — California as legally mandated (BR-2, BR-3), Germany with works council blocking requirement (BR-4), Indonesia as optional/business decision (BR-7). (2) Germany works council approval explicitly identified as a hard pre-condition via BR-4 with BetrVG §87(1)(6) citation and a feature gate mechanism. (3) Driver classification data model difference thoroughly identified — employee (CA) vs. contractor (ID) with separate deduction taxonomies (BR-8) and explicit escalation warning against conflation as misclassification risk. (4) Formal escalation for legal review present in multiple escalations, particularly the missing_context escalation recommending legal counsel review before implementation. (5) No single global acceptance criteria set — AC-1 through AC-7 are jurisdiction-specific. (6) confidence.level = 45, well within the ≤60 threshold.

observations.text: Exceptionally thorough jurisdiction analysis — goes beyond minimum requirements by identifying specific legal statutes (Labor Code §351, §226, BetrVG §87(1)(6), AB5) for each jurisdiction
Correctly identifies the deduction taxonomy conflation trap as a misclassification risk — this is the subtle data model concern the rubric requires
Works council requirement modeled as a concrete feature gate mechanism (BR-4), not just a mention — includes testable assertion that no German driver can access the feature while gate is pending

Identifies the subtle trap that Indonesia's optional status doesn't mean the feature can be removed globally — BR-7 explicitly notes it can be toggled off for Indonesia 'without legal consequence' while BR-2 makes California mandatory
Edge case for works council approving with modifications shows deep understanding — recognizes the works council may constrain what is displayed
Catches a requirement the scenario didn't even test for: overtime display for California employees under AB5, escalated as a likely missing requirement
Business impact correctly identifies phased jurisdiction-specific rollout as required and flags that Germany may be code-complete but legally unshippable for months
Confidence level of 45 with well-articulated reasoning about legal complexity, undefined terms, and works council uncertainty demonstrates appropriate epistemic humility
